flashcards.front: Define a recursive function in data structures.
flashcards.back: A recursive function is a function that calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller subproblems of the same type. It requires a base case (stopping condition) to terminate recursion and a recursive case that reduces the problem size. Recursion uses the call stack to store return addresses and local variables for each recursive call.

flashcards.front: What is linear search and its time complexity?
flashcards.back: Linear search is a simple search algorithm that checks each element sequentially in an array until the target element is found or the list ends. It works on both sorted and unsorted arrays. Time complexity is O(n) in worst and average cases, and O(1) in the best case when the element is at the first position.

flashcards.front: Write a recursive linear search algorithm in pseudocode.
flashcards.back: Function LinearSearch(arr, n, target, i): if i >= n return -1; if arr[i] == target return i; return LinearSearch(arr, n, target, i+1). Base case returns -1 if element not found; otherwise, it checks each index recursively until a match is found or the array ends.

flashcards.front: What is binary search and when is it applicable?
flashcards.back: Binary search is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that searches a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It compares the middle element with the target and eliminates the half where the target cannot exist. It only works on sorted arrays and has a time complexity of O(log n).

flashcards.front: Write a recursive binary search algorithm in pseudocode.
flashcards.back: Function BinarySearch(arr, low, high, target): if low > high return -1; mid = low + (high - low) / 2; if arr[mid] == target return mid; if arr[mid] < target return BinarySearch(arr, mid+1, high, target); else return BinarySearch(arr, low, mid-1, target). This recursively searches the right or left half based on comparison.

flashcards.front: Compare the time and space complexity of linear search vs binary search.
flashcards.back: Linear search: Time O(n), Space O(1) for iterative or O(n) for recursive call stack. Binary search: Time O(log n), Space O(1) for iterative or O(log n) for recursive call stack. Binary search is significantly faster for large datasets but requires a sorted array, while linear search works on unsorted data.

flashcards.front: When should you use linear search versus binary search?
flashcards.back: Use linear search for small arrays, unsorted data, or when performing a single search. Use binary search for large sorted datasets or when performing multiple searches, as O(log n) is much faster than O(n). Binary search is preferred for databases and dictionaries; linear search is better for simple lists.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using recursion?
flashcards.back: Advantages: Simplifies code for inherently recursive problems (tree traversal, factorial, Fibonacci) and improves readability. Disadvantages: Function call overhead increases time complexity, recursive calls use stack space (risk of stack overflow for deep recursion), and may use more memory than iterative solutions. Always consider iteration for performance-critical code.
